era_context: Early Iron Age. The Zhou Dynasty in China is in the reign of King Mu (traditional dates ~1001–922 BCE, though disputed), a figure celebrated in later legend for his travels and wisdom. Egypt's 21st Dynasty is in transition; priestly power at Thebes continues to grow. Phoenician maritime networks are at their height. In the Levant, the period following the Late Bronze Age collapse has stabilized into a network of Iron Age city-states and kingdoms.

events.title: Reign of King Mu of Zhou (traditional dating)
events.description: King Mu of Zhou is traditionally dated to rule during this period, with traditional chronologies placing his reign from approximately 1001 to 922 BCE. He is celebrated in later Chinese texts, particularly the Mu Tianzi Zhuan (Account of King Mu of Zhou), as a legendary figure who undertook a great journey westward to meet the Queen Mother of the West (Xiwangmu) in the Kunlun Mountains. Modern scholars debate whether Mu was a historical figure, a composite of multiple rulers, or a largely legendary character. Oracle bone inscriptions provide some evidence of a Zhou king named Mu, but do not confirm the legendary narratives.
key_figures: King Mu of Zhou (r. traditional dates ~1001–922 BCE, disputed)

sources.name: Mu Tianzi Zhuan (Account of King Mu of Zhou), compiled in the 4th century BCE or later
Shiji (Records of the Grand Historian) by Sima Qian
Oracle bone inscriptions mentioning 'Mu' as a Zhou king

events.certainty_note: Traditional chronology places Mu's reign in the early 10th century BCE, but modern scholars using oracle bone inscriptions and astronomical correlations have proposed significant revisions. The exact dates are uncertain by ±20–50 years. The legendary narratives about his western journey are almost certainly not historical.

disconfirming_evidence: The legendary account of King Mu's journey to the Kunlun Mountains and meeting with the Queen Mother of the West is almost certainly mythological rather than historical. Modern scholarship treats the Mu Tianzi Zhuan as a work of literature and legend, not reliable history. The figure of Mu may be historical, but the narratives surrounding him are heavily embellished or invented. Additionally, the traditional dating of Mu's reign has been challenged by modern chronological revisions; some scholars place his reign significantly later than traditional chronology suggests. The oracle bone evidence for Mu is fragmentary and does not provide precise dates.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 995 BCE is sparse and heavily dependent on later, unreliable sources. The Mu Tianzi Zhuan is a literary work compiled centuries after the events it purports to describe, and it blends historical, legendary, and mythological elements without clear distinction. The Shiji, while more systematic, was also compiled over 1,000 years after Mu's time and relies on earlier sources of uncertain reliability. Oracle bone inscriptions provide some contemporary evidence but are fragmentary and do not provide narrative detail. Chinese chronology for this period remains contested among scholars; traditional chronologies have been revised multiple times based on new archaeological and astronomical evidence. There is a strong bias in surviving sources toward the Zhou court and elite perspectives; the experiences of common people, women, and non-Han populations are almost entirely absent from the record.

graph_edges.note: Both Mu and Zhao are part of the Zhou Dynasty political structure, though their exact chronological relationship is disputed
